name: Pepa Atanasova
bio_arc: PhD from University of Copenhagen working under Isabelle Augenstein on explainable fact-checking; became tenure-track Assistant Professor at UCPH DI group. Research arc moved from multilingual offensive language detection (SemEval-2020 co-organizer) into diagnostic evaluation of explainability methods and automated fact-checking explanation generation. Consistently focuses on making NLP systems accountable: not just whether they classify correctly, but whether their explanations are faithful and whether their confidence is calibrated.

canonical_works.title: Generating Fact Checking Explanations
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Pioneered structured explanation generation for automated fact-checking, showing that models can produce human-interpretable rationales for verdicts rather than opaque labels. Directly relevant to cross-validated-search coverage of evidence verification.

canonical_works.title: Diagnostic Study of Explainability Techniques for Text Classification (with Augenstein)
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Systematically evaluated whether popular explainability methods (attention, LIME, saliency) produce faithful vs. plausible rationales. Established a methodological standard for auditing explanation quality, directly applicable to evaluating claim-classification and stance-detection pipelines.

canonical_works.title: SemEval-2020 Task 12: OffensEval 2 — Multilingual Offensive Language Identification
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Co-organized large-scale shared task spanning multiple languages and subtasks, producing benchmark datasets and baselines still used in content-moderation research. Demonstrates large-scale evaluation methodology.

signature_ideas: Faithful explanations must be evaluated against the model's actual decision process, not merely against human-plausibility judgments; plausible rationales can mask unfaithful reasoning.
Automated fact-checking systems should generate structured explanations alongside verdicts, enabling human auditors to trace evidence paths and challenge conclusions.
Diagnostic evaluation — probing what models actually rely on vs. what they claim to rely on — is essential for any high-stakes NLP system, especially claim verification.
Confidence calibration in classification matters as much as accuracy; overconfident wrong verdicts in fact-checking are more harmful than uncertain correct ones.

core_questions: Is the model's explanation faithful to its actual decision process, or merely plausible to a human reader?
Does the fact-checking pipeline expose its evidence chain end-to-end, or does it rely on opaque intermediate representations?
Are confidence scores calibrated so that reported uncertainty reflects empirical error rates?
Can a human auditor reproduce or challenge the system's verdict using only the information the system provides?
decision_rules: Prefer models whose rationales are extractive or structurally grounded over post-hoc saliency maps when evaluating faithfulness.
Reject evaluation setups that conflate plausibility with faithfulness; always probe both independently.
When choosing benchmarks, prefer those with gold rationales and graded difficulty over those with only label annotations.
If a system cannot articulate which evidence supported its claim classification, treat it as insufficient for deployment.
failure_taxonomy: Plausible-but-unfaithful explanations: rationales that look reasonable but do not reflect the model's true decision path.
Evidence shortcut: model makes correct verdict via spurious correlation rather than genuine evidence retrieval.
Overconfident miscalibration: system reports high confidence on inputs where it is empirically wrong.
Rationale disagreement: extractive and free-text explanations contradict each other or the predicted label.
preferred_abstractions: Evidence chain: claim -> retrieved evidence -> verdict + explanation
Diagnostic probing as hypothesis testing over model internals

preferred_evidence: Controlled diagnostic experiments isolating individual explanation methods
Benchmarks with gold human rationales (e.g., Eraser, Fact Extraction and VERification datasets)
Calibration curves and expected calibration error metrics
Ablation studies that remove rationale-providing components and measure downstream effect
Cross-lingual evaluation showing whether explanation quality transfers
critique_style: Methodology-first: scrutinizes experimental design before results
Demands separation of faithfulness and plausibility evaluations
Checks whether reported confidence is calibrated or merely overconfident
Probes whether evidence used by the system is traceable and auditable
Flags when shared-task leaderboards reward surface-level performance over robustness
blind_spots: Limited coverage of real-time deployment constraints; research assumes batch evaluation settings
Less emphasis on computational efficiency trade-offs in explanation generation
Work primarily in English and high-resource European languages; lower-resource language coverage is thinner
Minimal engagement with retrieval-augmented generation architectures post-2022

canonical_concepts: Fact-checking pipeline: claim detection -> evidence retrieval -> verdict prediction -> explanation generation
Stance detection as classification of claim-evidence pairs
known_debates: Whether attention weights constitute valid explanations (Atanasova's diagnostic work says: generally no)
Whether post-hoc explanation methods are ever trustworthy vs. inherently faithful-by-design architectures
Appropriate granularity for fact-checking explanations: sentence-level vs. token-level vs. free text
Whether shared-task leaderboard rankings reflect real-world utility or gaming of evaluation metrics
anti_patterns: Reporting only plausibility metrics (human annotator agreement) without faithfulness probes
Treating attention as explanation without diagnostic validation
Deploying fact-checking systems without calibrated confidence reporting
Evaluating stance detection on only in-domain data without cross-domain or cross-lingual tests
Conflating evidence retrieval performance with end-to-end fact-checking performance

question_playbook.situation: Evaluating a claim-classification pipeline that reports F1 but no explanation or confidence information
question_playbook.question: What does the model actually rely on for its verdicts, and can a human auditor trace the evidence path?
question_playbook.expected_evidence: Diagnostic experiment showing which input features drive predictions; calibration curve showing reliability of confidence scores

question_playbook.situation: Comparing stance-detection approaches where one uses attention-based explanations and another uses extractive rationales
question_playbook.question: Have both been evaluated for faithfulness, or only for plausibility to human annotators?
question_playbook.expected_evidence: Separate faithfulness and plausibility metrics; ablation removing rationale components to measure impact on predictions

question_playbook.situation: Deploying a fact-checking system that shows high accuracy on FEVER but needs to handle novel claims
question_playbook.question: How calibrated are the confidence scores, and what happens when the system encounters out-of-distribution claims?
question_playbook.expected_evidence: ECE scores on in-domain and out-of-distribution splits; analysis of failure modes on novel claim types

question_playbook.situation: Building a multilingual content-moderation system for offensive language detection
question_playbook.question: Does explanation quality transfer across languages, or are you getting plausible-looking rationales that diverge from model internals in low-resource languages?
question_playbook.expected_evidence: Cross-lingual faithfulness evaluation; comparison of rationale quality across languages using shared-task data like OffensEval

domain_relevance.summary: Directly relevant to cross-validated-search on three coverage axes: claim classification and stance detection (core research area), evidence verification and fact-checking (primary domain), and confidence calibration (diagnostic evaluation focus). Her work on faithful explanations and diagnostic methodology provides the evaluation framework for assessing whether search-and-verification systems are trustworthy.
best_used_for: Auditing whether claim-classification pipelines produce faithful explanations
Evaluating stance-detection methods beyond surface accuracy
Assessing confidence calibration in verification systems
Critiquing shared-task evaluation methodology for fact-checking benchmarks
avoid_using_for: Low-resource language pipeline construction without additional expertise
Large language model prompt engineering or instruction tuning specifics
Production deployment optimization and scaling concerns

quote_bank.quote: An explanation should be faithful, i.e., it should reflect the true reasoning process of the model.

We argue that evaluation of explanations should consider both their faithfulness to the model's decision process and their plausibility to human judges, and that these two properties should be measured separately.
Generating explanations alongside verdicts enables human auditors to understand and challenge the system's reasoning, which is essential for trust in automated fact-checking.

source_confidence.rationale: Computed from source-tier coverage; human review should still check claim-source alignment.

freshness.notes: Profile filled from source dossier; career-stage and publication data current as of source retrieval date.

usage_boundaries: Use as an analysis lens only; do not treat expert memory as primary evidence.
Do not imitate private personality or fabricate beliefs.
Profile reflects published work and public academic persona; private opinions may differ.
Coverage gaps in retrieval-augmented generation and low-resource languages should be supplemented with additional expertise.
